creator.name: Parker Walbeck
creator.oneLiner: Learn filmmaking fundamentals from Parker Walbeck's proven video techniques.
suggestedQuestions: How do I shoot professional interviews on a tight budget?
What camera and lens settings should I use for the best image quality?
How do I structure and edit my video to keep viewers engaged?
What gear and equipment does a beginner filmmaker actually need to get started?

chunks.text: All right, let's talk about how to shoot an interview with a budget of just $1,500. Now, I did this tutorial a few years ago with a ton of gear, a ton of budget, and the main thing I was seeing in the comments is, "Please do it with less expensive gear so it's more applicable to us." So, here we are. I went out and shopped out the least expensive gear that I recommend to be able to still get professional results. So, I'm going to show you how to use all this gear and shoot a professional interview that you could do for corporate interviews. This also applies to doing a talking head for a YouTube video or like a podcast. So, this is highly applicable to a wide variety of content. We're going to go through all the steps that I take when shooting a talking head like this from choosing the location where we're going to actually film it, the composition, how we want to frame it up, the lens choice, the focal length, the lighting. Lighting is huge. Then the camera settings, dialing in all the white balance, the shutter speed, all that. Then we'll get to audio. That's just as important as what you see, as what you hear. And then I'll show you the actual interview process. And then we'll go in post-prouction and show you how I piece this together, how I put it to music. Speaking of which, big thanks to Musicbed for sponsoring this video. They are making this possible. So stick around to the end. We'll show you how we integrate music to bring the interview to life and create emotion. Also back here, we've got our subject for the day. This is Callie Jeepson. She is interning this summer, so she's been nice enough to be our subject. That will be our mock interview. And Callie is a beginner and so she's going to be asking questions as we go. Also, Tanner behind the camera will be asking some questions to help you guys learn as we are going. But let's first talk about the gear I recommend buying to shoot a professional interview on a budget. The first thing you're going to need is a camera. We've got here the Canon R50V. This is about as inexpensive as I would go with a camera. Much less than this. You might as well just use an iPhone, whatever you got in your pocket. But this guy you can
chunks.videoTitle: How to Film Interviews (Under $1,500)

chunks.text: let's first talk about the gear I recommend buying to shoot a professional interview on a budget. The first thing you're going to need is a camera. We've got here the Canon R50V. This is about as inexpensive as I would go with a camera. Much less than this. You might as well just use an iPhone, whatever you got in your pocket. But this guy you can pick up for about $570 USD body only. Now, these cameras will usually have an option to come with a kit lens, but I do not recommend getting the kit lens. They usually cost around $100 to $200, and they're really not very good lenses, so you might as well put that money towards specific lenses for the specific needs you have. In this case, the lens we are using is a Sigma 30 mm RFS lens that goes down to a 1.4 aperture. Now, that is key is being able to have that low aperture. That's going to allow us to get a shallow depth of field or a lot of background blur today, whereas most kit lenses will not allow you to do that. So, this lens comes in at $345. Now, on the lens here, we have a KN&F variable ND filter, which isn't totally necessary. And again, I'll show you the difference with and without. But if you want to be able to keep your shutter speed low, which will allow you to keep the motion blur natural, then you will need one of those. That comes in at $33. Then you see the tripod we have here is a $53 tripod. This is actually one we used to make. We don't make it anymore, but there are a lot very similar to this. Beginner tripods do not have fluid heads, which make it harder to be able to actually film like live action on a tripod, which I do recommend, but if you don't have budget, you can get away with just a tripod like this if you're just doing static shots. This right here is our microphone we are choosing to use for the day. This is the Coma VM20. The nice thing about this is it's a microphone that you could put on your camera itself if you're just filming handheld. But if you get a nice boom pole like this, you can stick it on your camera and turn it into a boom mic that you can then use for interviews. Now, this boom arm comes in at $58. That's not cheap. But if you don't buy something like this, you're going to

But if you get a nice boom pole like this, you can stick it on your camera and turn it into a boom mic that you can then use for interviews. Now, this boom arm comes in at $58. That's not cheap. So, do we always need a light? Not necessarily. If you have in this case a lot of natural light streaming in that works as a soft. Look how big this light source is. That's acting as a giant key light for our subject here. The problem with depending on natural light is clouds come and go. And if you're only depending on natural light, cloud comes in, all of a sudden your exposure gets really dark and your interview's ruined. So, I don't recommend relying on natural light. But if that's all you have, it can look pretty decent right there, as you can see. All right. So, that's one option. How are we going to compose the framing of this? And in which situations are we having them look directly at camera versus off camera? For a typical interview, you want to put them on onethird of the frame. So right now, Callie is approximately on a third of the frame there. Her eyes are about on the top third and her face is about on one of the side thirds. And whichever way she's looking, you typically want to have more room in front than you do behind. That's called looking room. And then you want also some good head room, not too much space above or too much space below. You start framing her like that, then it gets kind of awkward. Like we're showing a bunch of the ceiling for no reason. or if we start getting down here and starting to crop her head out, that doesn't look good either. So, you want to have a healthy amount of space up here, but not too much. And one of the main reasons to have a good amount of

chunks.text: start framing her like that, then it gets kind of awkward. Like we're showing a bunch of the ceiling for no reason. or if we start getting down here and starting to crop her head out, that doesn't look good either. So, you want to have a healthy amount of space up here, but not too much. And one of the main reasons to have a good amount of space up there is for cropping vertically. So, we live in 2026. Half of our stuff we're doing is going to be ending up on vertical. So, I'm keeping that in mind where I'm like, "Okay, if I were to put this in a vertical frame on Instagram, if I'm putting her head at the very top, she's going to be crunched up there where the Instagram logos on her forehead, and there's just not much space to work with there. So, yeah, keep in mind that vertical framing. >> Would you ever frame them in the center for an interview?" >> When to frame them in the center? So, let's center her up and then I want you to look over here. To me, this feels like why are we centering her if she's not looking at us? If you look at me now, now I'm thinking, oh, it's symmetrical. She's in the middle and she's looking forward and her face is split in half. If she's in the center, but her face is only half showing, then it feels a little bit strange to me. When should they talk to the camera versus talk off camera? >> Yeah. So, for example, if you're doing a a corporate interview with a business owner who isn't comfortable talking to a camera, which is most people, most people are terrible at talking to a camera. So, in that scenario, you might say, "Okay, hey, we're going to have you read a script and look at the camera." They they're not good at reading scripts. They feel awkward looking at a camera and it's going to come across in a way that feels overproduced and awkward. Whereas, if you have one of their good friends over here and they're just having a conversation and you're just capturing that conversation and it feels natural and raw and organic, then you might say, "You know what? In this situation, it's probably best to have them talking to somebody in a conversation, a dialogue, rather than making it feel like they're lecturing the audience and speaking directly to

chunks.text: just having a conversation and you're just capturing that conversation and it feels natural and raw and organic, then you might say, "You know what? In this situation, it's probably best to have them talking to somebody in a conversation, a dialogue, rather than making it feel like they're lecturing the audience and speaking directly to them. So, it's also context. Who are they talking to? Who's the audience? I am talking to you. you are my customer and I'm here to say something to you versus I am talking about my business in general and why I think it's good for other people, not you, but just people in general. And that kind of takes the tension off of the audience of like, oh, I don't feel like I'm being lectured. I get to just be a fly on the wall and observe a conversation. You can still say the same types of things, but now you're talking to people instead of you. >> And then are your eyes eye level with the camera? Like are you higher or >> Yeah. So, as far as height goes, typically you want to be eye level. You can see right now I'm a little bit below eye level. So, we could raise this up a little bit. But one of the things to consider with how high is what you want to have in your frame. So, watch this as we lower this. And if I'm like, I don't really want to see the ceiling, then we need to bring it up a little bit. And now I'm not seeing any ceiling, but now I'm starting to cut off the lights above her a little bit. So, this is more of a question of what do I want to see in the background? Anywhere near eye level is going to feel natural. I personally would like to see a little bit more of those lights. How far away should we be from the subject? This kind of comes down to how many cameras do you have? In this case, we have one camera. I would say something like this. I would say generally a medium shot, which is going to be waist up, is going to be your typical distance to be away. This is more of a tight shot. If you have two cameras, this would be a nice angle for a tight shot. One camera, I think that's too tight. I think we need to get more of a medium shot. The fact that I only have one camera, I'm going to kind of split the difference and do a medium

chunks.text: to be waist up, is going to be your typical distance to be away. This is more of a tight shot. If you have two cameras, this would be a nice angle for a tight shot. One camera, I think that's too tight. I think we need to get more of a medium shot. The fact that I only have one camera, I'm going to kind of split the difference and do a medium shot, but a little bit tighter of a medium shot. So, I can still crop well for vertical, but we're still nice and close and intimate without being too far away where I'm feeling removed. I don't have a second tight shot to cut to. So, I kind of need to be somewhat tight. When do you know if it's necessary to use a second camera and a tight shot? >> Yeah, I mean I would say typically having two cameras is ideal. What you do with those two cameras is usually the A camera, the main camera is going to be somewhere in the medium range. If I had a second camera, I'd either choose to do a tight or I do a wide. More typical is to give you a tight. And the reason for a tight is because you get to see a little bit more of their emotion. It's nice to be able to have a tight shot to cut to when we have an intimate part of the interview. We really want to show what they're saying and the all the expression and emotion on their face versus wide is is more of going to be just showing location, which if you have a third camera, I'd get a wide, but I do it in that order. Medium, tight, wide would be the order in which I would choose to set up angles. >> Do you need the tight shot to be at a different angle? >> Yep. And because we only have one camera today, we're not going to get into this a lot, but your second angle should be at least 30 degrees from your subject. If you get too close, it's going to start feeling like a jump cut. Now, if you're changing focal length, then that can still work. If you're only this far off, 15°, but you're a lot tighter, then it's not going to feel too jump cuty. So, main thing to keep in mind when setting up a second camera angle is being about at least 30 degrees away and picking a different focal length, being tighter or wider. What I want to hit on next is our lens choice. While we're here, lens choice is also a part of

chunks.text: off, 15°, but you're a lot tighter, then it's not going to feel too jump cuty. So, main thing to keep in mind when setting up a second camera angle is being about at least 30 degrees away and picking a different focal length, being tighter or wider. What I want to hit on next is our lens choice. While we're here, lens choice is also a part of composition, but we're going to make that its own little category here. For lens choice, we are using a 30 mm lens on an APS-C sensor camera, which means this has a 1.6x crop. A full-frame camera has no crop, but an APS-C sensor, which is usually the type of sensor you're going to need to use if you want to be on a budget because they're a lot cheaper, has usually about a 1.6 crop, which means your lens is going to crop in like that. So, a 30 mm actually looks like a 48 mm. So, you do the math. 30 * 1.6 equals 48. Different focal lengths are going to give different appearances on the face. So, we have a whole video in the course showing what a focal length does to the face, stretching it out, making it feel more like clownish and then zooming out and making everything feel more compressed. So, a 50 mm is kind of in the middle. If you go all the way to like 16 mm wide, it's going to distort the face if you get really close. And 200 mm, it's going to flatten everything out. 50 is right in between. In a medium framing situation, 50 is about what our eyes see. So, it's very natural for our eyes to see um this amount of compression and distance. So, if you're going to pick one lens for interviews, I would pick a 50 mm on full-frame or around a 30 mm on an APS-C sensor camera. Tanner asked, can you use that 50 mm for a tight shot? You can, but as we just talked about, she's starting to look unnatural at this focal length. It's distorting her features a little bit. her nose is coming out and feeling longer than it actually is. And so I would never personally use a 50 mm uh focal length for a tight shot. About as low of a focal length as I would go is around 70 mm. Typically you're going to be between about 70 and 100 mm for that tight angle there. >> Yeah. What's the widest focal length you'll go for a medium shot? >> Uh the widest I'd probably go is a 35.
chunks.videoTitle: How to Film Interviews (Under $1,500)

chunks.text: would never personally use a 50 mm uh focal length for a tight shot. About as low of a focal length as I would go is around 70 mm. Typically you're going to be between about 70 and 100 mm for that tight angle there. >> Yeah. What's the widest focal length you'll go for a medium shot? >> Uh the widest I'd probably go is a 35. As long as you're not getting too close, 35 is still going to look pretty natural being at a medium shot. Uh once you start getting much beyond 35, you're having to get unnaturally close to her to get that kind of framing. If you were to use a wide shot, I'd probably use something like a 24 mm. 24 to 35 is usually good. And then you just have to get a little bit further back and then you show their all their feet and everything. But again, we're just doing one camera angle, keeping it simple. So, for this video, focusing just on that medium shot, which I recommend being around 50 mm. All right, that's it for focal length. Let's now talk about lighting. Lighting is the thing that makes or breaks an interview to make it look professional or look amateur. So, please pay attention and take this part serious. This is crucial and this is what most beginners neglect. They think, I'm just going to show up, turn on the camera, turn on the mic, and we're good to go. And they don't know what to look for to know if the lighting is looking good. And so we're going to talk about some of the things to look for. Now, typically I want her being brighter than most the background. There's pockets of light. So, we have these pendants up here and we have, you know, some lights splashing on on the the backsplash of the kitchen there. So, I like those pockets of light that are just as bright, if not brighter than her. But generally, I like the rest of the background to be a little bit darker than her, so that she pops out and it's easy to tell that we want the audience to be focused in and looking at her. So, let me show you an example. If I turn off this light, you're being lit by natural light, but I'm going to block the natural light for a minute. Then, we have to bring up the exposure here. And now, you can see that the background is brighter than her. I'm focused on the
chunks.videoTitle: How to Film Interviews (Under $1,500)

chunks.text: easy to tell that we want the audience to be focused in and looking at her. So, let me show you an example. If I turn off this light, you're being lit by natural light, but I'm going to block the natural light for a minute. Then, we have to bring up the exposure here. And now, you can see that the background is brighter than her. I'm focused on the bright stuff behind her. The countertops are brighter than her. The the lights behind her are brighter. and she's kind of shadowed. So, that's an example of having flat light, light that just doesn't help her stand out from the background. Okay. So, Cali, talk to me about where I should put this lights. Why not put it right above you? Or why not put it completely over here? What's the difference between where I'm positioning this light? That's what happens when you're not high enough. But if you went too high, then you're going to start getting what are called raccoon eyes and you can start to see that her eyes are looking shadowed. And this is more of like a interrogation scene type lighting, which that might be the look you're going for. Kind of the middle ground for most interviews. We want it to be above her, but not so far above her that we are making her eyes turn dark. Okay. Okay. So, I'm going to turn this up a little bit so you can see more of what's going on here. Get a little bit more contrast going on. Typically, you want this light to be about 45° from your camera or sorry, 45° from your subject. So, if the camera's over here, but she's looking here, then the light has to go even farther because her face is facing this way. We want the light to be 45° from the way she's facing. The reason for that is we're looking at this drop shadow right here. We want to create that drop shadow, that darkness on one side of the face. And we're having one side lit up and the light is wrapping around the face and falling off on the cheekbone. Now, what that's doing is as we're falling off on the cheekbone, that accentuates the cheekbone and allows her to feel more

chunks.text: drop shadow right here. We want to create that drop shadow, that darkness on one side of the face. And we're having one side lit up and the light is wrapping around the face and falling off on the cheekbone. Now, what that's doing is as we're falling off on the cheekbone, that accentuates the cheekbone and allows her to feel more shapely. So, beneath the chin here and on the cheek here is all falling into shadow. And that's called a drop shadow. And that just helps create dimension on the face. So we call that lighting direction. The direction at which you have the light coming from is really important because it's going to shape the face in a way that's either flattering or not flattering. Now let's talk about light quantity or the amount of light. So if I bump this all the way up to 100, this light ratio of light to dark is very contrasty. This is a high contrast light ratio. If we bring this down to like 10%. So now there is there is some shadow going on here. The contrast between light and dark is very little. So that's a low contrast image. I tend to be again somewhere in the middle. I want to be overly dramatic, but I like a little bit of contrast. I feel like it feels more cinematic, if you will. And so if I pump that up to around 30%. Now I feel like we have a decent amount of contrast while not being overly dark in the background or overly contrasty on her face. Last thing to talk about with light in general with this key light. Talked about light direction. Talked about light quantity. Now let's talk about light color. So what color is this light, Cali? >> Around like 56. >> 56. You nailed it. K 5600 Kelvin replicates daylight. So, what this is doing is trying to just add to the fact that she's being lit by window light. Those lights in the kitchen are at 3,200 Kelvin or 3500 around there. So, what we have going on between the key light and the background light is a difference in light color. This is a bluer light at 5600 and the background light is at 3200, which is a warmer light. Why would you want two different colors of light in your image? >> For color contrast. >> Color contrast. She know she's already taken the course. She's nailing it. >> So color contrast. The reason you want

chunks.text: the background light is a difference in light color. This is a bluer light at 5600 and the background light is at 3200, which is a warmer light. Why would you want two different colors of light in your image? >> For color contrast. >> Color contrast. She know she's already taken the course. She's nailing it. >> So color contrast. The reason you want that is that helps create depth. So if all the lights were the exact same color, which there's times you do that and that's fine. But if you want to add more contrast, you can add different colors. So she is one color, the background is another color. And that helps divide those two layers and create differentiation or contrast between foreground and background. So, we create depth in a bunch of ways, but one of the ways is by shaping the light in her face. One of the ways is by making her brighter than the background in general, by separating her from the background. Depth happens by looking for leading lines, layering things. And then the last way we create depth is by that shallow depth of field of the lens that puts everything out of focus. But before we get to camera settings, we're going to add one more light. Now, you could get away with just this one light. This looks great. Don't feel like you have to add a hair light, but I want to now show you what happens when we bring a hair light in. If there was something right here that was being lit up, and we didn't want this to be lit up, just her. That's where you would want to buy something like barn door so that you can make sure that only the light's hitting her. All right. So, we've got our hair light over here. This is the Halo 60X. Nice thing about this is it's an amaran light which pairs up with the situs link which means I can control it from my phone. Okay. So again, the reason why we have this stand here is because if this stand was here, it would be in frame right behind her. So now I have this just out of frame right here. So this frame comes all the way out here and then you can't see it because it's coming all the way out of

Now, in a traditional interview setting like this, you'll often hear the phrase threepoint lighting. What that usually means is you have your key light that lights your subject, a hair light or backlight, and then a feel light that fills in any of the shadows over here. And instead of buying a third light, I often just recommend a 5 in1 reflector that you can pick up for like 30 bucks. Don't need this. But if you want a little bit more control on your lighting, you pick one of these up. And now watch Callie's face as I bring this in. This is without any fill light. Shutter speed is set to 150th, which is going to be double the frame rate, which is 24 frames per second. I prefer shooting at 24. If you shoot in 30, it's going to look more like a news broadcast. 60 is more for slow motion. 24 gives you more of a movie look. So 24 frames per second, we're shooting in 4K. If you're going to shoot in log because you want to be able to manipulate the color grade and get the maximum dynamic range, that's great. Most beginners, I don't recommend it. So we are shooting in Canon 709. And an important setting to help you dial in the exposure on your subject is I like to come into my zebras. I like to set my zebra one level to plus or minus 60. And what that does is it'll show zebras on the part of the face, the kelet part of the face of my subject to help me know when they're in the proper exposure range. plus or minus 60, which means when it reaches that level, 60 out of 100, 100 being pure white, zero being black, 60, around 60 is a good range to be for skin tones. Uh, really anywhere from 70 to 50 is kind of the skin tone range. For Caucasian skin, they're going to be more in the mid60 to 70 range. So now, when I turn this zebra on, you can see that those zebras are showing up on the kelet side of her face, and that lets me know if I'm in the right exposure range. So you see right here there's no zebras. That means we're too dark. Zebras start to appear and then zebras start to go away. That means I'm getting too bright. So we're just trying to get to a point where we're seeing zebras on that whole part. And once they start to go away, that's about where you'd stop. And that means you're at a correct exposure level. chunks.text: All right, so we got here the Canon R6V. Just got announced today and Canon sent me one early so that I could test it out, get my hands on it, and give you my first impressions. [music] I think this sits right between the C50 and the R6 Mark III. I don't own that camera, so I'm going to kind of be comparing it to my 5D Mark [music] II and the C50. Those are the cameras I own. But basically, what this is is a new line of camera [music] where there isn't photo first, it isn't cinema, it's kind of in between. It's video first, but stealing features from cinema and photo line. And honestly, I've been waiting a while for Canon to make a segment like this. I'm really excited where they go with this segment. They did the R50V introducing this V [music] line, and this is the first full-frame version of the V line, and it is jam-packed with features. However, there are some things to be aware of that it doesn't have that you're still going to get out of the cinema line or the R line with the photo first. Now, talking to Canon, they said that this was built specifically for social media video content creators, but the upper tier of that customer base, the more professional level. And so, they've included some cinema features and some photo features. So, we'll talk about where this sits and the features it gives you. So, let's start out with IBIS in-body image stabilization. This is the thing that people were hoping would come in the C50. It did not, and I'm happy to say that the R6V does come with IBIS. However, it is not the same IBIS that you'll find in the R series. In the R6 Mark III or the R5 Mark II, it is a different IBIS, and this is important. The R series IBIS is going to give you eight stops of stabilization, whereas this R6V is going to give you seven stops. So, it is a slightly downgraded version of IBIS. I don't know why that is or what that means exactly, but in my limited testing, it did seem to perform slightly worse at stabilizing compared to my R cameras. It felt a little bit more jittery, whereas the R cameras felt

chunks.text: In today's tutorial, I'm going to be showing you how to add text behind your subject using the all new object mask tool inside of Premiere Pro. Let's dive in. Step one, you're going to grab your clip and duplicate it. I like to hold option and just drag the clip to layer number three, and all the work we'll be doing is on that top clip, and I'll explain why we duplicate it later. Step number two is you're going to come in here to your toolbar and select the object mask tool, and then hover over your subject and select your subject. Step number three is to add any additional objects you want to your mask. So, for me, I want to make sure we add our microphone, but I can add these canisters, my keyboard, and if I click on those things, they become part of my mask. Now, we don't want those. I'm going to come in here and select minus and deselect those objects. But, we do want to add this microphone, and sometimes you can see these little dots appearing. Every little dot signifies a border, and we don't want a border. With the lasso tool selected, draw small lassos around all the borders. And when the dots go away, you'll know that they are now part of the mask completely. Step number four is to now track your mask. So, if you'll come over here to your unassigned masks section in your effects, you'll see that we can track backward and forward or forward, which is nice knowing you don't have to be at the beginning or the end of your play head. You can be anywhere on your clip and just track forward and backward. Hit track, and as you can see, it now will start to track our mask beautifully. You're going to now grab your object mask and drag it down into opacity. What you're doing now, if I make the bottom clip invisible, is you're moving the background from everything that isn't that mask on that top layer. Now, we can put things between our subject and our background. So, we're going to come in here and add text. We'll say full-time filmmaker. Now, drag your text in between your mask layer and your background layer. If I drag the text to the side of my head, you'll really start to see the imperfections, but it's not awful, easy to fix. So, come into your mask and you

chunks.text: and our background. So, we're going to come in here and add text. We'll say full-time filmmaker. Now, drag your text in between your mask layer and your background layer. If I drag the text to the side of my head, you'll really start to see the imperfections, but it's not awful, easy to fix. So, come into your mask and you have three options. You have your feather, your opacity, and your expansion. All I'll be doing is adding feather at 5%. If you go too much, you'll start to lose the mask. If you don't do any at all, it looks a little too sharp. So, we're going to come in and we're going to do 5.8. Let's play it back. Looks pretty good. There's a little imperfection on my finger here. So, what we're going to do is we're going to come in here and we're going to keep with our plus selected and draw a little circle around my finger. Perfect. All right, so we are liking the mask. It looks great. Text looks good. It's feathered nicely. Now, there is still some pixelating on my shoulder and on my face, which we don't want. But, we can refine just a little bit more, and so I want to show you how to do that. So, first, you're going to want to nest your top layer, your mask, come into your effects, and search for roughen edges. And surprisingly, roughen edges will smooth your edges. Now, we're going to drag it onto this top clip. Make sure it's nested and watch the skin. In 3, 2, 1, boom. Look at how much refinement happens on that edge. Now, because it added some sharpness where we didn't want it, we're going to lower that edge sharpness to 0.70. And there we have our final result. And you're done. Whole thing takes about a minute. Now, you can do a lot more fun things to really spice this up. Like, for example, you could add Gaussian blur to your background text to help match the bokeh, so that way it really looks like the text is in the background and not just right behind my subject. But, there you have it. That is how you put text behind objects in Premiere Pro using the object mask tool. Now, coming soon and currently available in the beta version of Premiere Pro is the smooth versus sharp object mask track, which we won't get into today, but just know it's on its way. And if

chunks.text: the background and not just right behind my subject. But, there you have it. That is how you put text behind objects in Premiere Pro using the object mask tool. chunks.text: We've got here the DJI Mavic 4 Pro and the DJI Mini 5 Pro, and we're going to be doing a head-to-head video shootout to show you how these two stack up and the different reasons why you'd want to buy one over the other. Now, I've come up with 23 different categories that are important to me when buying a drone, and I'll be assigning each drone a point depending on which one I think performs [music] better in that category. However, each category is not weighted the same. come to your own conclusions based on your needs as to which categories are most important [music] to you. Also, this video is not sponsored by DJI. They don't care which one you buy. I don't care which one you buy. They're both great. I'm just here to help you decide which one is right for you. This video is, however, sponsored by Storylocks. So, first, I'm going to be showing you a blind test of both drone footage side by side to see if you can tell the difference. And while you're watching that footage, I'm going to be answering a question that I get asked weekly, and that is what site do I use for my stock footage? Now, for years, at the top of my list for what I recommend and use is Story Blocks. And there's three main reasons why. Number one, in my experience, they have the widest selection of stock video clips. When I enter [music] a keyword searching for a specific clip, more than any other site I use, they have the greatest chance of having what I'm looking for. Number two [music] is for the giant library of stock footage, music, sound effects, and motion graphics that are included. I believe they offer the best bang forbuck pricing. And number [music] three is you get unlimited downloads for that price. There's no hidden fees or extra fees. Plus, their library is frequently being added to based on the latest trends of what members are searching for. So, that huge library just keeps growing. So, to get started with unlimited stock video downloads at [music] one set price, head over to storyblocks.com/fulltimemaker or you can click the link down in the description below. Also, leave a comment below on which drone you liked better, A or B. And let's now reveal which was which. Drone A was the Mini 5 Pro and

chunks.text: just keeps growing. So, to get started with unlimited stock video downloads at [music] one set price, head over to storyblocks.com/fulltimemaker or you can click the link down in the description below. Also, leave a comment below on which drone you liked better, A or B. And let's now reveal which was which. Drone A was the Mini 5 Pro and drone B was the Mavic 4 Pro. So, if that's all you watch in this video, I think it's safe to say that you can get professional results with either one. But let's now pixel peep and break down the difference between the two. Our first category is weight and size. The Mavic 4 Pro's takeoff weight comes in at 1,63 g or 2.3 lb, whereas the Mini 5 Pro weighs around 250 g. So, the Mavic 4 Pro is about four times heavier than the Mini 5 Pro. And this is honestly one of the biggest reasons to get the Mini 5 Pro is just the travel friendliness of it. So, point here for the Mini 5 Pro. Our next category is loudness. Another advantage of the smaller size of the Mini 5 Pro is that it's quieter. I tested the loudness of both hovering in the air about 5t away and the Mavic 4 was around 67 dB and the Mini 5 was 61 dB. And here's how they sounded so you can hear for yourself. So, if you want to be more discreet and draw less attention, the Mini 5 Pro is the clear winner again, as it's definitely quieter. However, size has its downfalls because the lighter the drone, the shakier the footage will be in high wind. So, how do they each handle in windy conditions? Now, on paper, they're both rated at a maximum wind resistance speed of 12 m a second, or about 27 mph. But those numbers don't mean that the shots will be smooth in those windy conditions. It just means it can withstand that without being blown off course. So I put both of them up in the air during some high wind conditions around 30 mph. And just taking a static shot, the Mini 5 Pro was slowly getting pushed off of its framing while the Mavic 4 Pro held its framing the whole time. So for pro users who don't want to be limited as much by the wind, the Mavic's weight will actually be a benefit here. So point goes to the Mavic 4 Pro. Next, we have remote functionality and screen quality. Mavic
chunks.videoTitle: DJI Mavic 4 Pro vs DJI Mini 5 Pro // Which Drone Should You Buy?

chunks.text: shot, the Mini 5 Pro was slowly getting pushed off of its framing while the Mavic 4 Pro held its framing the whole time. So for pro users who don't want to be limited as much by the wind, the Mavic's weight will actually be a benefit here. So point goes to the Mavic 4 Pro. Next, we have remote functionality and screen quality. Mavic 4 Pro offers the new RC Pro 2, which is a 7-in rotatable monitor at 2,000 nits brightness, and the Mini 5 Pro offers a 5.5 in 1080p touchscreen that features 700 nits of brightness. Now, keep in mind, you can pair the larger 7-in remote with the Mini 5 Pro, but there is no package option that comes with it, so you would have to buy it separately. But here's a look at them side by side. The Mavic 4 Pro is just going to be easier to see due to the brighter and larger screen. However, the Mini 5 Pro screen was plenty bright outside as well. I didn't have any problem seeing either one. But for optimal visibility, I definitely prefer using the larger and brighter 7in screen. Also, a convenient feature, when you lift the Mavic 4 screen from the controller, it automatically turns on and then off again when you put it back down. Plus, when you rotate the screen, that automatically puts the gimbal into vertical shooting mode, making it super quick and easy to switch and giving you the full size of the screen to see that vertical image. And we'll talk more about vertical filming later, but overall advantage here for the Mavic 4 Pro controller and screen. It is a very cool design. Our next category is practicality. This is similar to weight and size, but it's so important that I think it deserves its own category. Because no matter how good a camera or a drone is, it's not of much use unless you actually take it out and use it. And a big factor as to whether you use a drone is how easy or practical it is to use. Now, both of these have very similar startup times as far as setting it up and turning it on. Both are easy to operate, and both are userfriendly. They each have similar functionality and controls. However, because the Mini5 Pro is more on a proumer level with less advanced features, less weight, smaller size, it's just going to be easier for

chunks.text: use. Now, both of these have very similar startup times as far as setting it up and turning it on. Both are easy to operate, and both are userfriendly. They each have similar functionality and controls. However, because the Mini5 Pro is more on a proumer level with less advanced features, less weight, smaller size, it's just going to be easier for the everyday user to haul around on a vacation, take on a hike, grab some quick B-roll shots for a YouTube video. So, if you're new to drones or need something that's meant for daily use or taking on an airplane, for example, the Mini 5 Pro is a great tool to have in your bag. And even if you plan on owning the Mavic 4 Pro like I do, there are still times that I prefer shooting on the Mini 5 Pro, and I'll talk about some of those later. So, point here again for the Mini 5 Pro. Next category is sensor size. The Mavic 4 Pro's main camera sensor features a Hasselblad 100 megapixel 4/3 in CMOS sensor. Then the medium camera is a 1 over 13 inch CMOS sensor. And then the telephoto lens is a one over 1.5 inch cmos sensor. Whereas the mini 5 Pro sports a 50 megapixel 1 inch CMOS sensor. And this is an upgrade from the Mini 4 Pro that had a 1 over 1.3 in sensor. And the quality bump in my opinion was very noticeable. But the Mavic 4 Pro still wins with the overall bigger sensor. And we'll talk about how the sensor size affects image quality in later categories. Starting with our next category, which is low light. The Mavic 4 Pro sports dual native ISO fusion, but the Mini 5 Pro can stop down to f1.8 versus the F2.0 on the Mavic 4 Pro. So, when you look at them side by side, you need to raise the ISO on the Mavic 4 Pro to match the brightness of the Mini 5 Pro. And when shooting in Dlog M, the Mini 5 Pro can only go up to 3200 ISO, while the Mavic 4 Pro can go up to 6,400 ISO. So, when maxing out both ISOs, the exposure matches pretty well. But if you look closely, there's less noise in the Mavic 4 Pro, and the overall image quality is sharper and less mushy. So, a point goes to the Mavic 4 Pro. And on a side note, here's a look at the Mavic 4 Pro's DLOG M versus DLOG. As you can see, the DLOG M performs better in low light than DLOG. Also, in normal picture

chunks.text: exposure matches pretty well. But if you look closely, there's less noise in the Mavic 4 Pro, and the overall image quality is sharper and less mushy. So, a point goes to the Mavic 4 Pro. And on a side note, here's a look at the Mavic 4 Pro's DLOG M versus DLOG. As you can see, the DLOG M performs better in low light than DLOG. Also, in normal picture profile, you can go all the way up to 128,000 ISO on both drones. But I found that when you increase the exposure in post, the DLOG M actually has less noise overall and has higher dynamic range. So, Mavic 4 Pro wins the best low light, but specifically in DLOG M. And speaking of dynamic range, that is our next category. The Mavic 4 Pro's dynamic range depends on which lens you use. On the main 28 mm lens, DJI claims 15.7 stops. On the middle 70 millimeter lens, they claim 14 stops. And on the 168 millimeter tightest lens, they claim 13 stops versus the Mini 5 Pro claims up to 14 stops. So about the same as the middle lens of the Mavic 4 Pro. Now, I'm not going to do any scientific tests, but here's a look at a sidebyside of the two main lenses in some harsh lighting environments, so you can see how they stack up. As you can see, there's better highlight and shadow retention on the Mavic 4 Pro, but I was very impressed with the Mini 5 Pro. Coming from the Mini 3 Pro, I felt that dynamic range was too weak on that drone for many pro situations, whereas this new Mini 5 Pro is much improved, but still a win here for the Mavic 4 Pro. And side note, the YouTube channel Make Art Now did some extensive tests on the Mavic 4 Pro and found that you can actually get the best dynamic range results when shooting in DLOG M in auto ISO. So, just an interesting note there if you're trying to maximize dynamic range. And that brings us to our next category, which is color profiles. The Mavic 4 has standard DLOG M, DLOG, and HLG, while the Mini 5 Pro is missing Dlog. DLOG on paper is going to be your best dynamic range option for maximum exposure and color flexibility in post to better match your on ground cameras. So, the Mavic takes the point here for having more color profile options. However, again, given Make Art Now's dynamic range test and

chunks.text: DLOG M, DLOG, and HLG, while the Mini 5 Pro is missing Dlog. DLOG on paper is going to be your best dynamic range option for maximum exposure and color flexibility in post to better match your on ground cameras. So, the Mavic takes the point here for having more color profile options. However, again, given Make Art Now's dynamic range test and given my low light test, there is an argument to be made that sometimes it's better to use DLOG M. So, just something to keep in mind there. But moving on to our next category is resolution and frame rates. The Mavic 4 Pro can shoot 6K at up to 60 frames per second and 4K at up to 120 frames per second, though it does have a slight crop. And on the other smaller cameras, it can do 4K at up to 120 frames per second, while the Mini 5 Pro maxes out at 4K at up to 120 frames per second. And when you put the 4K images side by side, you can actually see that they stack up pretty well. It's only when you zoom in by about 400% that you can see there's a little more compression going on in the Mini 5 Pro. But when you stack it up to the 6K image on the Mavic, there is a clear difference in overall clarity. So, the Mavic wins this category with an overall sharper and cleaner image. So, if you're needing that extra clarity and flexibility in post to crop in, Mavic is the way to go. But keep in mind that the 6K footage is a lot harder on your computer and obviously takes up more data. So, I'd plan on using proxies if you do shoot in 6K. Moving on to our next category is bit rate and overall image quality. The Mavic 4 Pro offers H.264 at 90 megabits per second and standard H.265 at 180 megabits per second. But if you get the 512 GB crater combo, the alli codec offers a significantly higher bit rate of up to,200 megabits per second, whereas the Mini 5 Pro gives you a max bit rate of 130 megabits per second. So, higher bit rate options on the Mavic 4 Pro, which will provide a cleaner image with less compression and artifacting. So, when you combine the higher bit rate of the Mavic 4 Pro with its larger 6K sensor, more picture profiles, you get a less compressed, higher detail, higher dynamic range image. So, I'd say this is the main reason to buy a Mavic 4 Pro

chunks.text: rate options on the Mavic 4 Pro, which will provide a cleaner image with less compression and artifacting. So, when you combine the higher bit rate of the Mavic 4 Pro with its larger 6K sensor, more picture profiles, you get a less compressed, higher detail, higher dynamic range image. So, I'd say this is the main reason to buy a Mavic 4 Pro over the Mini 5 Pro is if you're shooting high budget commercials, luxury weddings, luxury real estate, you better be showing up to that shoot with a Mavic 4 Pro to provide your client with that next level image quality. Could you get by with the Mini 5 Pro's image quality? Of course. But if you're getting paid top dollar, I'd say your clients deserve top quality. So, point here goes to the Mavic 4 Pro. Next category is lens options. The Mavic 4 Pro has three lenses, a 28 mm, 70 mm, and 168 mm, whereas the Mini 5 Pro has one lens at 24 mm. It does also offer a 2x zoom, but realize that that's going to be a digital crop combined with some software enhancement, not an actual separate optical lens. And as you can see here, it's clearly a big difference in image quality between the 70 millimeter middle lens of the Mavic 4 Pro and the digital 2x zoom of the Mini 5 Pro. So, if you want to be able to have more of that compressed zoomin look from your drone, big point here to the Mavic as it provides three lens options versus one. But big butt here for the main lens, I actually prefer the wider 24mm lens of the Mini 5 Pro. The Mavic 3 Pro had a 24 millimeter on the main lens, and I like that field of view better for those wide landscape or architecture shots, but I know some people prefer that more compressed look of 28 mm. So, it just depends on what you're filming. But, I'm going to give one point for the Mavic for three lens options, but half a point here for the Mini 5 Pro for having that wider field of view, which I personally like better. Moving on to our next category is aperture adjustability. This is a big one for me and a big reason why I don't love using the minis in pro situations because being able to adjust aperture just gives you more control in different lighting situations to quickly make adjustments to exposure while
chunks.videoTitle: DJI Mavic 4 Pro vs DJI Mini 5 Pro // Which Drone Should You Buy?

chunks.text: wider field of view, which I personally like better. Moving on to our next category is aperture adjustability. This is a big one for me and a big reason why I don't love using the minis in pro situations because being able to adjust aperture just gives you more control in different lighting situations to quickly make adjustments to exposure while maintaining that smooth motion blur with your shutter speed staying at double your frame rate. So, the Mavic 4 Pro's main camera has an adjustable aperture from 2.0 all the way up to f11 and then a fixed 2.8 aperture on the other two lenses, while the Mini 5 Pro is fixed at an F1.8. 8. So point here goes to the Mavic, but keep in mind that changing your aperture will change the sharpness of the image. Optimal sharpness will be had between about 2.8 and 4.0. Going any higher than that and you start to get a slightly softer image due to a phenomenon called defraction. And here's a look at f2.8 versus f11. So you can see that slight softening that happens. So just keep that in mind that you can adjust the aperture, but there is a small image quality trade-off. But moving on to our next category is vertical mode. They can both shoot in true vertical mode, which is where the gimbal flips the whole image, so you don't have to crop. So, that's an awesome feature on both. However, on the Mavic 4 Pro, the downward tilting is very limited, making it almost unusable in many situations. Whereas, on the Mini 5 Pro, you have way more range of motion, being able to tilt all the way down. Just this past week, I was shooting a project for a client where I needed vertical video from the air, but most of the shots I needed were looking down. And so my only option was the Mini 5 Pro. So, I'd say if you shoot primarily vertical video, the Mini 5 Pro is actually the better option. So, big point here to the Mini 5 Pro. But on the flip side, you have upward tilting, which is our next category. The Mavic 4 Pro can tilt up to 70° while the Mini 5 Pro can tilt up to 55°. This is an awesome feature to have to get low to the ground and be able to look up without seeing your props in the shot. So, it still allows for some good rolling, but doesn't quite have the latitude of the Mavic 4 Pro. Mavic 4 Pro features front-facing LAR sensor and 0.1 lux night omnidirectional avoidance, meaning you'll be able to avoid obstacles even in the dark. Next is transmission and range. The Mavic 4 Pro has the OcuSync 4 Plus transmission system at up to 30 km, while the Mini 5 Pro has OcuSync 4 transmission with up to 20 km. So, the Mavic gets the point here for 50% longer range. However, keep in mind that this range is clear line of sight. If you have buildings or anything obstructing that transmission, you'll get far less than that. I flew the Mavic out maybe half a kilometer, but as soon as there was an object between me and the drone, the feed lost connection completely. Now, luckily the drones are smart enough and they will return to home until they get that line of sight and transmission is restored. But just understand that those ranges are clear line of sight. But our next category is flight time. The Mavic 4 Pro claims 51 minutes of flight time. But in my testing on a full battery, moving it around constantly and not just hovering in place, I got about 32 minutes before the low battery warning came on and told me to land. And upon landing, it had 15% battery left. So, real world scenario, I think you can bank on 35 to 40 minutes. Whereas the Mini 5 Pro claims up to 36 minutes with a standard battery. And again, in my testing on a full battery, also flying it around the whole time, I got 22 minutes before the low battery warning came on and then landed it with 15% battery left. So, approximately 50% more flying time with the Mavic 4 Pro. That is quite the boost in flight time and a great perk of the much larger batteries that are provided by the Mavic 4 Pro. So, big point here for the Mavic. Next category is top speed. How fast can these go? Mavic 4 Pro has a claimed top speed of 25 m a second or 56 mph in sports mode. And the Mini 5 Pro in sports mode can go 18 m a second or 40 mph. So, if you do a lot of car chase filming, the Mavic 4 Pro is going to be much more useful for that fast-paced

chunks.text: So, big point here for the Mavic. Next category is top speed. How fast can these go? Mavic 4 Pro has a claimed top speed of 25 m a second or 56 mph in sports mode. And the Mini 5 Pro in sports mode can go 18 m a second or 40 mph. So, if you do a lot of car chase filming, the Mavic 4 Pro is going to be much more useful for that fast-paced scenario. So, again, point here to the Mavic 4 Pro. And then our next category is tracking features. Both drones have the newest updated Active Track 360, which does awesome subject tracking, and to my testing, they both did a great job. The Mavic 4 Pro will probably be better at tracking long distances or in high speeds. While the Mini 5 Pro, because of its smaller size, will do better in areas where there's more obstacles to fly between. So, I don't think there's a clear winner in this category. They're just going to perform better in different scenarios. So, we'll give a point to each. Now, side note, I would rarely use the active track on a professional shoot, but the spotlight mode I do actually think is very professional to use. it just locks in on your target and allows you to move the drone wherever you want while keeping your subject in the center of the frame. So, I think the spotlight is a very helpful professional feature while the active track it's a little jittery when it's trying to track something and seems to jump around and make the shots in my opinion not very usable. And moving on to our last category and a very important one is price. None of these features matter if you can't afford it. And again, that's one of the biggest reasons to get the Mini over the Mavic is affordability. Obviously in the US right now it's hard to even buy a DJI drone period. And hopefully those international relations get resolved quickly, but right now the Mavic 4 Pro starts at $3,000 USD. But for the highest creator combo option that I have here, it's going to cost you around $5,000 USD. Whereas on the Mini5 Pro, this starts at around $900 USD. And for the Fly More Combo, which is what I have here, that comes with the RC2 controller, it's going to be around $1,300. But because they aren't really selling them directly to the USA right

And for the Fly More Combo, which is what I have here, that comes with the RC2 controller, it's going to be around $1,300. But because they aren't really selling them directly to the USA right now, you're going to have to pay more than that. I personally bought my Mini 5 Pro from a friend who got it from a friend who bought it in Canada, and I ended up paying $1,700. But the point is, the Mavic 4 Pro is going to cost roughly two to three times more than the Mini 5 Pro. So, this factor alone may be the deal breaker for you that makes you go with the Mini 5 Pro. So, overall, no surprise that the Mavic 4 Pro wins in more categories, as it should, being the more expensive drone. But if the categories that the Mini 5 Pro won are more important to you, then hopefully this video saves you some money by letting you know that this is going to get you what you want. In summary, if price, weight, size, a wider field of view, vertical shooting, and overall practicality are the most important factors to you, then I'd say go with the Mini 5 Pro. But if price is not an issue and you just want the best image quality, more professional features like longer flight time, longer range, faster speeds, more lens options, better battery life, more color profiles, better wind handling, then the Mavic 4 Pro is the way to go. So hopefully that helps you decide which drone is right for you. We did not include the Air series in this comparison, but they obviously sit somewhere between these two. So if you want to compromise between the extremes, the Air series might be best for you. Personally for me, I'll be using the Mavic 4 Pro on all my high-end commercial work. But for clients who need that vertical content specifically or for vacation or tighter spaces where I need that wider field of view, I'll still be pulling out that Mini5 Pro. So, I was at a crossroad. Do I let it die or do I try and get somebody in here to keep running it? And I looked around thinking who who is going to take over a business that is mostly my face and my style of teaching and is built around me. And really the only two people that came to mind was Jake and Landon, the other two faces of full-time filmmaker over the years. Now, at this point, Jake had already been running with Runaway Vows, building out his own wedding course [music] and channel, and he was doing great. And so it wasn't really a good fit for me to be like, "Hey, come over and take this back over." He was doing awesome. Landon was also doing [music] awesome building out and running studio, his own video production company. And so I really didn't think there was a fit. But in the summer of 2023, Landon invited me to be on his podcast. And at the end of that conversation I had with him, we just got chatting to kind of gauge where each other were at. and we just realized that we were in a place where he was kind of looking for something bigger and different to do. I was in a position where I was trying to get somebody else to run the business. And so it just seemed like a good fit and a good time

chunks.text: conversation I had with him, we just got chatting to kind of gauge where each other were at. and we just realized that we were in a place where he was kind of looking for something bigger and different to do. And so at the end of 2023, about 2 years ago, Landon and I officially agreed that he would take over the business. But because he didn't have the capital to buy it, we worked out a revenue-based financing deal for him to pay for it over time.
